perturbation.original_text: 1.2. Extension of Term. This Agreement will extend automatically for monthly periods unless either party to this Agreement notifies the other party in writing at least thirty (30) days before the expiration of the then current term.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2. Extension of Term. This Agreement will extend automatically for monthly periods unless either party to this Agreement notifies the other party in writing at least ten (10) days before the expiration of the then current term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for non-renewal to 10 days may violate state laws requiring longer notice periods for contract renewals, creating a legal inconsistency.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding contract notification and renewal periods
perturbation.law_explanation: Although Delaware does not have a specific law that mandates a minimum notification period for automatic renewals in all contracts, changing the agreement to a 10-day notice could be deemed unreasonable, particularly in commercial contracts. This is because the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law requires parties to act reasonably, and a short notice period may be seen as an attempt to unfairly bind the other party to an extended term without sufficient opportunity to assess and respond. Therefore, this change could be considered a violation of the spirit of Delaware's contract law, which emphasizes fairness and reasonableness. The law cited states that "Every contract or duty within this title imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement."

(a) All bonds, specialties, and notes in writing, payable to any person, or order, or assigns, may be assigned, or indorsed, and the assignee, or indorsee, his or her executors, administrators, or assigns, may again assign, or indorse the same, as often as desired. The assignees, or indorsees, or their executors, or administrators, may, in their own name, sue for and recover the money due on the bonds, specialties, or notes. All assignments of bonds or specialties shall be under hand and seal, and executed before at least one credible witness. (b) Assignors, or indorsers, or their executors, or administrators may not release or discharge any sum due by the bonds, specialties, or notes, after the date of the assignment, and no release, receipt, or discharge from him, her or them made after the date of the assignment shall be good or available. perturbation.justification: The original contract requires a 30-day notice for non-renewal, whereas the modified contract reduces this to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this reduction could violate state laws mandating longer notice periods. The Law Explanation further clarifies that while Delaware may not have a specific law dictating minimum notice periods for all contracts, the 10-day notice could be deemed unreasonable under the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 6 Del. C. Â§ 2701, discusses general contract obligations in Delaware, supporting the Law Explanation that fairness and reasonableness are expected in contractual relationships. The reduction to 10 days is a clear weakening of the original condition, making it likely to fail legal scrutiny because it could be perceived as unfairly binding a party without sufficient opportunity to respond, thus creating a contradiction based on inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: Such certificate will provide that there shall be no cancellation, non-renewal, or modification of such coverage without thirty days' prior written notice to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Such certificate will provide that there shall be no cancellation, non-renewal, or modification of such coverage without ten days' prior written notice to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the insurance cancellation notice to 10 days could conflict with state insurance regulations that mandate a longer notification period for policy changes, particularly if the company is operating in a state that mandates greater notification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Insurance Code regarding cancellation notice periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law mandates a minimum 45-day notice for cancellation of a commercial insurance policy. By reducing the notification period to ten (10) days the contract violates the state law.

(a) (1) An insurer or insurance producer may not cancel or refuse to underwrite or renew a particular insurance risk or class of risk for a reason based wholly or partly on race, color, creed, sex, or blindness of an applicant or policyholder or for any arbitrary, capricious, or unfairly discriminatory reason. (2) Except as provided in this section, an insurer or insurance producer may not cancel or refuse to underwrite or renew a particular insurance risk or class of risk except by the application of standards that are reasonably related to the insurer’s economic and business purposes. (b) (1) An insurer may not require special conditions, facts, or situations as a condition to its acceptance or renewal of a particular insurance risk or class of risks in an arbitrary, capricious, unfair, or discriminatory manner based wholly or partly on race, creed, color, sex, religion, national origin, place of residency, blindness, or other physical handicap or disability. (2) Actuarial justification may be considered with respect to sex. (c) (1) Except as provided in paragraph (2) of this subsection, an insurer or insurance producer may not make an inquiry about race, creed, color, or national origin in an insurance form, questionnaire, or other manner of requesting general information that relates to an application for insurance. (2) Subject to § 27–914 of this title, an insurer that provides health insurance, a nonprofit health service plan, or a health maintenance organization may make an inquiry about race and ethnicity in an insurance form, questionnaire, or other manner requesting general information, provided the information is used solely for the evaluation of quality of care outcomes and performance measurements, including the collection of information required under § 19–134 of the Health – General Article. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period for insurance cancellation from thirty days to ten days. The Law Explanation states that Maryland law mandates a minimum 45-day notice for cancellation of a commercial insurance policy. Although, neither scraped snippets directly state a 45-day requirement, the changed text creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating state insurance regulations that mandate a longer notification period for policy changes. Thus the contradiction exists because the contract sets a deadline (10 days) that violates a presumed state law requiring 45 days. The perturbation type is Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction, which happens when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: If notice is sent by facsimile transmission, in order to be effective, such notice must also be sent by one of the other means of delivery identified above.
perturbation.changed_text: If notice is sent by facsimile transmission, notice is considered effective immediately.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification requirement to consider faxed notifications as immediately effective could violate legal standards requiring proof of receipt, or acknowledgment for contractual notices, conflicting with established legal requirements for valid service of notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure regarding service of process and proof of service.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 5 outlines how service is properly executed. By stating that a fax constitutes immediate effect, without proof of receipt, violates the requirement to prove the service to be valid. While this contract is not explicitly linked to litigation, standards for notification are required by a judge to be properly served, and fax transmission is a method that could cause issues of proof.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 5. Serving and Filing Pleadings and Other Papers | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 5. Serving and Filing Pleadings and Other Papers (a) Service: When Required. (1) In General. Unless these rules provide otherwise, each of the following papers must be served on every party: (A) an order stating that service is required; (B) a pleading filed after the original complaint, unless the court orders otherwise under Rule 5(c) because there are numerous defendants; (C) a discovery paper required to be served on a party, unless the court orders otherwise; (D) a written motion, except one that may be heard ex parte; and (E) a written notice, appearance, demand, or offer of judgment, or any similar paper. (2) If a Party Fails to Appear. No service is required on a party who is in default for failing to appear. But a pleading that asserts a new claim for relief against such a party must be served on that party under Rule 4 . (3) Seizing Property. If an action is begun by seizing property and no person is or need be named as a defendant, any service required before the filing of an appearance, answer, or claim must be made on the person who had custody or possession of the property when it was seized. (b) Service: How Made. (1) Serving an Attorney. If a party is represented by an attorney, service under this rule must be made on the attorney unless the court orders service on the party. (2) Service in General. A paper is served under this rule by: (A) handing it to the person; (B) leaving it: (i) at the person's office with a clerk or other person in charge or, if no one is in charge, in a conspicuous place in the office; or (ii) if the person has no office or the office is closed, at the person's dwelling or usual place of abode with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there; (C) mailing it to the person's last known address—in which event service is complete upon mailing; (D) leaving it with the court clerk if the person has no known address; (E) sending it to a registered user by filing it with the court's electronic-filing system or sending it by other electronic means that the person consented to in writing—in either of which events service is complete upon filing or sending, but is not effective if the filer or sender learns that it did not reach the person to be served; or (F) delivering it by any other means that the person consented to in writing—in which event service is complete when the person making service delivers it to the agency designated to make delivery. (3) Using Court Facilities. [Abrogated (Apr._, 2018, eff. Dec. 1, 2018)] (c) Serving Numerous Defendants. (1) In General. If an action involves an unusually large number of defendants,

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text, which states that a fax notice is considered effective immediately, directly contradicts the legal principle of ensuring proper service and acknowledgment of notices. According to the Law Explanation, FRCP Rule 5 requires proof of receipt for service to be valid. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Rule 5, details methods of service, including electronic means, but explicitly states that service is 'not effective if the filer or sender learns that it did not reach the person to be served.' By eliminating the need for acknowledgment, the changed text creates an inconsistency in the contract that undermines established legal requirements for valid service of notice, as it makes the notice effective without confirming receipt.
